# How I build things (and why it matters)

Think of this as my personal "how I work" guide — not some formal business document, but a friendly explanation of my approach to building software and engaging with the developer community. I believe the best work comes from passion, curiosity, and treating people with respect.

## 🎯 What I'm about

I'm here to create useful things! Through open source contributions, sharing knowledge, and genuine collaboration, I build solutions that hopefully make other developers' lives a bit easier and maybe solve some interesting problems along the way.

Everything I do is guided by five core principles that keep me focused:

1. **Transparency** — No hidden agendas! I believe in clear communication and open decision-making that everyone can understand.
2. **Quality** — I take pride in my work, from clean code to helpful documentation to treating community members with respect.
3. **Inclusivity** — Different perspectives make everything better. I want everyone to feel welcome to contribute and be part of the conversation.
4. **Innovation** — I love experimenting and trying new approaches, but I won't break things just for the sake of being different.
5. **Responsibility** — I'm not just writing code; I'm participating in a community. That's something I take seriously.

## 🤝 How I approach projects

Since it's just me working on these projects, I wear all the hats! But I try to approach each responsibility thoughtfully:

**🚢 Project ownership** means I make the big-picture decisions, set technical direction, and try to keep everything moving forward. I've learned that good planning prevents most problems, and when issues do come up, addressing them quickly is usually better than letting them linger.

**🔧 Day-to-day maintenance** is where I spend most of my time. I review contributions (and genuinely appreciate them!), manage releases, handle issues, and keep documentation current. I try to be responsive and helpful, even when I'm juggling multiple projects.

**🎭 Community interaction** is something I really enjoy. I want people to feel welcome when they interact with my projects, whether they're reporting bugs, suggesting features, or contributing code. Creating a positive environment is just as important as the technical work.

### How I make decisions

I'm a big fan of thinking things through! For smaller changes, I usually just implement them if they make sense. For bigger decisions that could significantly change how a project works, I try to document my reasoning and often ask for community input through issues or discussions.

When I'm considering major changes, I like to think about: what problem I'm trying to solve and why it matters, how I plan to approach it (with enough detail that others can understand), what alternatives I considered, and what impact this might have on existing users.

## 📂 Managing my projects

I work on different types of projects, each with their own approach:

### Open-source projects

My public repositories are where I try to make my best work available to everyone. I make sure they have:

- Documentation that actually makes sense (I test it by explaining it to non-technical friends)
- Clear contribution guidelines, so you know exactly how to help out
- Automated testing — because nobody likes broken software
- Regular security check-ups (better safe than sorry)
- Up-to-date dependencies and proper licensing

I welcome contributors of all skill levels! Whether you're experienced or just getting started, I'm happy to help you get involved. I try to be responsive, ship updates regularly with clear release notes, and maintain public roadmaps so you can see what I'm planning next.

### Personal and experimental projects

My private repositories are where I work on more experimental ideas, personal tools, or projects that aren't quite ready for public consumption yet.

Even though they're private, I try to maintain the same standards — code review (even if it's just me taking a second look), documentation for future me, and all the usual good practices. Some of these eventually become open source once they're more mature or I think they might be useful to others.

## 🔄 How I build things (with care and attention)

I'm pretty particular about code quality, but it's all in service of creating things that work reliably and don't surprise anyone with unexpected behavior.

**Here's my approach:** Even working solo, I try to take a step back and review my code before committing it to the main branch. I rely heavily on automated tests (computers catch things I miss), and I always update documentation when I add new features. I take time to think through security implications because nobody wants nasty surprises.

I protect my main branches — no direct commits! Everything goes through pull requests, even if it's just me. I run security scans automatically and keep dependencies updated with regular vulnerability checks.

For releases, I use [semantic versioning (SemVer)](https://semver.org/) — a consistent way of numbering releases so users know what to expect. I write detailed changelogs that explain what actually changed (no vague "bug fixes" entries).

## 👥 Building community

I love celebrating people who make my projects better! Contributors get highlighted in release notes and documentation, and I'm always happy to help connect people with speaking opportunities if they're interested. Early access to new features is a small way I can say thanks to active community members.

While I don't have formal mentorship programs (being just one person), I do try to be helpful to folks who are getting started with open source. I believe in clear communication about how to get involved, and there are always opportunities for community members to take on more responsibility if they're interested.

### How I stay connected

**💬 Public communication:** GitHub Issues and Discussions for feature requests and questions, occasional social media updates about project news, and sometimes I participate in community calls or meetups when time allows.

**📞 Direct contact:** For more sensitive topics or detailed discussions, people can reach me at [william@hallin.media](mailto:william@hallin.media). I also maintain a website at [hallin.media](https://hallin.media/) with more information about my work and background.

## 🛡️ Security and doing the right thing

Security isn't just about fancy tools — it's about creating a safe environment for everyone and handling people's trust responsibly.

I have a clear process for [reporting security issues](SECURITY.md) (because sometimes bad things happen), conduct regular security audits on my projects, run automated dependency scanning, and have plans for handling incidents if they occur.

On the legal side, I maintain clear licensing for all public projects and respect third-party licenses and dependencies. When needed, I handle contributor agreements properly and try to stay on top of relevant regulations.

### Privacy matters

I design with privacy in mind, comply with data protection regulations, and handle any user data with care. I try to stay current with security best practices since this landscape is always evolving.

## 📊 How I measure success (and keep improving)

I'm a bit of a data nerd in the best way! I track various metrics to understand how my projects are doing and where I can improve:

**💚 Community health:** How diverse and engaged my contributor base is, how quickly I respond to issues and pull requests, feedback from users through surveys and direct communication, and overall project activity and engagement levels.

**⚡ Technical performance:** Code quality metrics and keeping technical debt manageable, how fast I respond to security vulnerabilities, release frequency and stability, and performance monitoring for projects where it matters.

**🔄 Continuous improvement:** I do periodic reviews of how things are going (usually quarterly), gather feedback from active community members, refine my processes based on what I learn, and try to adapt to changing technology and community needs. I invest time in improving documentation and sharing knowledge, and I enjoy learning about new technologies and approaches.

---

_Got questions about how I work, need clarifications, or just want to chat? Drop me a line at [william@hallin.media](mailto:william@hallin.media) or create an issue in the relevant repository. I genuinely enjoy talking about this stuff and hearing from people who use my projects!_
